AtomicFact.description: A single atomic fact extracted from a source.

direct_quote.description: Exact quote from source supporting this fact
direct_quote.title: Direct Quote

fact_category.description: Category: statistic, measurement, causal_link, named_entity, date_time, regulatory_threshold, standard_reference, geographic, methodology
fact_category.title: Fact Category

relevance_score.description: Relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
relevance_score.title: Relevance Score

confidence.description: Confidence that this fact is correctly extracted, 0.0 to 1.0
confidence.title: Confidence

perspective.description: STORM research perspective: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends
perspective.title: Perspective

entities.description: Key named entities (compounds, methods, organizations, metrics) mentioned in this fact. Used by the wiki mesh entity canonicalization pass. Optional; legacy data defaults to [].
entities.title: Entities

AtomicFact.title: AtomicFact

SourceAnalysis.description: Analysis of a single source document.

source_type.description: Type: journal_article, government_report, industry_report, news, standard, patent, book, other
source_type.title: Source Type

overall_relevance.description: Overall relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
overall_relevance.title: Overall Relevance

atomic_facts.description: All atomic facts extracted from this source
atomic_facts.title: Atomic Facts

evidence_summary.description: Brief summary of what this source contributes
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

SourceAnalysis.title: SourceAnalysis

system:
You are a research evidence analyst. Extract atomic facts from source material.

Rules:
1. Extract 8-15 atomic facts per source. Focus on the MOST relevant, specific, and unique claims, statistics, findings, and conclusions. Quality over quantity — prioritize facts that DIRECTLY answer the research question over tangential details. Missing a highly relevant fact is worse than including marginally relevant ones.
   CRITICAL: ONLY extract from the ARTICLE BODY TEXT. NEVER extract from: navigation menus, cookie consent banners, URL paths/slugs, abbreviation/glossary tables, author byline metadata, page footers/headers, table of contents page numbers, product category labels, or marketing taglines. If the source is mostly non-content (nav, ads, cookie text), extract ZERO facts and set source_quality to 0.0.
2. Each atomic fact = ONE specific claim with the EXACT quote from the source (minimum 10 words, up to 200 chars). A quote shorter than 5 words is NEVER acceptable — skip that fact entirely.
3. Source quality: Score each source individually on 0.0-1.0 based on:
   - Peer review status (peer-reviewed journal > preprint > report > blog)
   - Author credentials (named experts > organizations > anonymous)
   - Recency (2023-2026 > 2018-2022 > older)
   - Specificity to research question (directly relevant > tangentially related)
   Do NOT use fixed scores for source types. Vary scores based on each source's specific qualities.
4. Rate relevance to the SPECIFIC research question on 0.0-1.0:
   - 0.9-1.0: Directly answers part of the research question with specific data
   - 0.7-0.8: Provides important context or supporting evidence
   - 0.5-0.6: Tangentially relevant or provides background
   - 0.1-0.4: Marginally relevant, mostly off-topic
   Each fact must get a UNIQUE relevance score. No two facts from the same source should have identical scores.
5. Include exact numbers, dates, measurements, names, standards.
6. For EVERY quantitative finding, extract ALL of the following when available:
   - Effect size with units (MD, SMD, WMD, OR, HR, RR)
   - 95% confidence interval (e.g., "95% CI -5.54 to -3.05")
   - Heterogeneity (I-squared value, e.g., "I²=96%")
   - Number of studies/trials included (e.g., "7 RCTs")
   - Total sample size (e.g., "n=269")
   - Evidence certainty/quality rating (e.g., "GRADE: moderate", "high certainty")
   - Study duration/follow-up period
   Missing any of these when the source provides them is a CRITICAL extraction failure.
7. For review/meta-analysis papers, extract findings from EACH cited study individually.
   Prioritize pooled estimates with I² and CI over narrative summaries.
8. For each atomic fact, tag the research perspective it belongs to.
9. CRITICAL (ARCH-4): The 'statement' field must be a DIRECT PARAPHRASE of the source text.
   Do NOT infer, extrapolate, or add information beyond what the direct_quote states.
   If the direct_quote is fewer than 10 words, the statement must be equally narrow in scope.
10. QUOTE LENGTH: The 'direct_quote' field MUST be 150 characters or fewer.
   Quote only the KEY phrase, not entire sentences or paragraphs.
   This forces you to identify the most important fragment of evidence.
   BAD: "A systematic review and meta-analysis of 24 randomized controlled trials totaling 1,768 participants found that alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   GOOD: "alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   A 2-word quote like 'distilled water' can only support a statement about distilled water,
   not a full sentence about healthcare settings or bacterial contamination.
   Perspectives: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends.
   Choose the single best-fit perspective.

Output format (return ONLY this JSON structure):
{"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://example.com", "source_title": "Study Title", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.8, "overall_relevance": 0.7, "year": 2024, "authors": ["Smith J"], "venue": "Water Research", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "E. coli was detected in 30% of tested filters after 6 months", "direct_quote": "E. coli presence in 30% of tested POU filters", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.9, "confidence": 0.8, "perspective": "Scientific"}], "evidence_summary": "Bacterial contamination in POU water filters"}]}
prompt:
Research question: What catalysts have demonstrated the highest Faradaic efficiency for electrochemical CO2 reduction to ethylene since 2022?

Analyze the following 2 sources and extract the TOP 8-15 MOST
relevant atomic facts per source. Focus on the most specific, unique, and
directly relevant numbers, dates, measurements, claims, findings,
regulations, and standards. Quality over quantity.

Sources:
Source URL: https://www.jnsam.com/article_218580.html
Source title: Safe-by-Design Approach for Next-Generation Electrochemical CO₂ Conversion Prototype with Risk Assessment and Safety Considerations
Source type: academic
Content:
Document Type : Research Article Graphical Abstract Highlights Keywords 1. Introduction The safe-by-design concept is based on a variety of underlying principles, including green chemistry, circular chemical engineering, life cycle assessment, responsive research and innovation, and sustainable development[1,2]. It encompasses various aspects of process safety, personal safety, and broader implications. The EU Safe and Sustainable by Design framework emphasises that safety should be the highest priority, followed by environmental sustainability, and then social and economic sustainability as shown in figure 1. While safety assessments can be conducted once a system is in place, integrating safety considerations during the early stages of development (safe-by-design) is crucial to alleviating roadblocks in later stages. Environmental assessments can be performed using life cycle assessment (LCA), while social and economic sustainability evaluations can be conducted through techno-economic assessments and Socio-Technical Integration Research (STIR)[3,4]. A safer process, a safer workspace, and a reduction in the likelihood of injuries are the most immediate benefits of the safe-by-design approach [5,6]. Its principles influence the economic, environmental, and social aspects of an activity, process, or industrial sector. Since the development of a safe-by-design framework requires thorough planning and a deep understanding of the process, it encourages stakeholders to critically analyse and identify potential flaws. Following safe-by-design principles leads to a comprehensive understanding of each step involved and its implications for process, personal, and environmental safety[7]. This careful consideration and identification of safer alternatives foster innovation. Furthermore, this deeper understanding improves the usability of components, enhances productivity, and can ultimately reduce process costs [6]. Implementing a safe-by-design approach also facilitates compliance with organisational health and safety regulations and government legislation. Overall, safe-by-design benefits processes, individuals, and organisations while ensuring compliance and cost-effectiveness. This concept is widely applied across various industries, including engineering, manufacturing, and technology, to develop products, processes, and systems that prioritise safety from the inception[8–10]. By integrating safety considerations into the design phase, companies can proactively mitigate risks, minimise the likelihood of accidents or failures, and enhance overall reliability[10]. In engineering, safe-by-design principles are used to develop structures, machinery, and infrastructure that can withstand environmental and operational stresses while ensuring the safety of users and workers [10]. In manufacturing, these principles help in designing safer production lines, minimising exposure to hazardous materials, and implementing automation to reduce human error. In the technology sector, safe-by-design is crucial for developing secure and reliable hardware and software, ensuring data protection, cybersecurity, and user safety [11,12]. Beyond these industries, safe-by-design is also applied in healthcare, pharmaceuticals, and energy sectors, where maintaining high safety standards is essential for regulatory compliance and public trust [13]. By embedding safety considerations at every stage of development, organisations not only protect individuals and the environment but also improve efficiency, reduce costs associated with accidents or failures, and foster innovation in safer and more sustainable technologies [14]. 2. Safe-by-design in NEXTCCUS context The NEXTCCUS project (Next Generation Electrochemical System for Sustainable Direct CO₂ Capture and Utilisation/Storage as Clean Solar Fuel) is an ambitious initiative aimed at developing an innovative, renewable energy-based technology to convert captured CO₂ into methanol—a sustainable, versatile liquid fuel [15]. By integrating advanced electrochemical processes with renewable energy, the project seeks to achieve efficient CO₂ conversion under ambient conditions while reducing environmental impacts. A critical aspect of the project is the implementation of a safe-by-design approach, ensuring that safety is prioritised throughout every stage of development to operational phases—from material selection and reactor design to operation and long-term environmental management. For electrochemical fuels production, the development of electrochemical processes at the electrodes and overall system optimisation is of central importance[16,17]. Currently, we are focusing on developing the anode, cathode, membrane, and reactor at the laboratory scale, with risk assessments already conducted for these components (see the single stack components in Figure 2). This report is aimed at the transition to a pilot-scale system, using a scaled-up design as the next target. The system utilises a waste CO₂ stream from a point source, which is then processed in our stacked system consisting of 10 × 10 cm² individual units (10 stacks), with a total active area of 1000 cm². The safe-by-design framework for this development follows the guidelines outlined in the EU Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) report (Figure 3). The framework begins with a thorough review of existing processes and safety protocols. Next, the boundary conditions for the new system are defined, followed by the selection of appropriate methods, tools, and indicators. These indicators are then applied to the system to develop standardised procedures and establish criteria for safe operation and performance evaluation. By adhering to the safe-by-design philosophy, electrolyser-based fuel production can be implemented with a strong emphasis on safety, ensuring the protection of both operators and the surrounding environment (Figure 4). The method emphasises on extensive discussions and early interventions based on the specific process, preliminary data, and assessed risks. In this early intervention, a five-step approach is required for the safety assessment. These steps are the identification of the hazards, followed by the classification and characterisation of the hazards during operation. The next step is to identify health and ecological hazards of the biproducts during the lifetime, exposure assessment, and then risk characterisation. In the following section, we discuss the long-term implications of using various components and their potential impact on system safety and sustainability. This analysis will provide insights into how each component contributes to the overall safety and efficiency of the system, highlighting areas for potential improvement and innovation. In the context of electrolysers—devices that use electrical energy to produce fuels such as methanol and hydrogen (H2)—the “safe-by-design” approach can be divided into several key sections. These sections will cover the design principles, materials selection, operational protocols, and maintenance strategies that ensure the safe and sustainable operation of electrolysers. 3. Risk Assessment and Management Risk assessment involves identifying the risks inherent in the process and determining strategies to mitigate them [19]. This evaluation can be divided into two parts: 3.1. Preliminary Risk Assessment The purpose of this stage is to identify potential hazards in the process and design strategies to minimise them. 3.1.1. Materials and Chemicals Selection This involves selecting the materials for the anode, cathode, membrane, and electrolyte. Based on NEXTCCUS research, we plan to use earth abundant copper, nickel and iron (CuNiFe) as anode, a Cu2NCN catalyst [22] on a carbon gas diffusion electrode (GDE) as cathode, a tintanium carbide (Ti₃C₂) MXene as membrane, and 1 M potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte. As shown in Table 1, hazard information for the anode and cathode catalyst materials is not available. Therefore, additional data is needed to assess the risks associated with these novel, materials-based electrodes. The major risk arises from the presence of flammable and toxic gases, as well as methanol, which are inherent in the product stream and cannot be entirely avoided. 3.1.2. Electrochemical Reactor Selection Selecting the appropriate reactor involves evaluating both the materials and the geometry, as these factors significantly affect system safety. We plan of implementing flow reactor that incorporates dedicated chambers for the anode, cathode, and gases. For simplicity in fabrication, we are considering constructing the reactor from Perspex. A reactor of 1000 cm2 at 200 mA cm-² current density is assessed in this work. 3.1.3. Operational Hazards This is the most critical aspect of the safe-by-design assessment, encompassing several key considerations. Please refer to Figure 5 for an overview of the various hazards. Below is a detailed description of the identified hazards: Electrical Shock Hazard: Our target operation is at 200 mA cm-² over an active area of 1000 cm², which results in a total current of 200 A. With an operating potential between 2 and 3 V, the electrolyser delivers a total power of 400–600 W. This level of current and power is significant and necessitates rigorous insulation and safety protocols to prevent electrical shock. Gas Leak: In the CO₂ reduction process, CO₂ gas is injected, and a significant portion of the products are in the gaseous phase. Assuming an 80% faradaic efficiency for CH₃OH production, the remaining 20% is split equally between H₂ and CO (10% each). At an operating condition of 200 mA cm-² over 1000 cm², the reactor is estimated to produce approximately 0.995 moles of CH₃OH per hour and 0.373 moles each of CO and H₂ per hour. Converting these amounts, about 40.25 ml of CH₃OH and 8.35 liters of CO and H₂ per hour are produced. These calculations use Faraday’s law (where FE is the faradaic efficiency, Z is the number of electrons required per product molecule, and F, 96485 C/mol, is Faraday’s constant). In this reaction, methanol production requires six electrons, while CO and H₂ production require 2 electrons each. With an assumed 60% carbon utilisation efficiency, the required CO₂ flow rate is approximately 851 ml/min. Given these significant production rates, it is imperative that effective precautions are to be taken to manage gas leaks. Liquid Leaks: In our CO₂ reduction system, which uses an anion exchange membrane (as opposed to a bipolar membrane), hydroxide ions are consumed at both the anode and cathode. This necessitates a reservoir to replenish the consumed hydroxide and maintain the correct pH. Additionally, since the reactor outlet will contain a mixture of methanol and KOH, proper precautions must be in place for safe product collection and subsequent distillation. Optimising the KOH flow rate is essential to ensure a high concentration of methanol, which facilitates easier distillation and lowers downstream energy costs. Gas Crossover Issues: The crossover of gases between the anode and cathode chambers can present serious operational risks. This is particularly significant in our system, where MXene membranes are still in the research phase. The accidental transfer of oxygen into the cathode chamber or H2 into the anode chamber could create a flammable mixture, thereby posing explosion hazards. It is therefore essential to measure the gas crossover by utilising gas chromatography and, adjust the membrane fabrication and reactor operational parameters accordingly before further scale-up of the system. Pressure Management: Proper pressure management is critical for safe operation. Although we plan to operate the reactor at atmospheric pressure—thus making inlet pressure less critical—the outlet pressure can become problematic due to CO₂ utilisation and the generation of various gases. Localised pressure build-up may also enhance gas crossover through the gas diffusion electrode (GDE) or membrane. Hence, careful analysis and control of outlet pressure are required. 3.1.4. Automation and Monitoring Automating reactor operations—including power supply control and CO₂ flow regulation—will ensure smoother and safer operation. Continuous monitoring of CO₂ inlet and outlet pressures, as well as current and potential, is essential for quickly identifying and addressing any anomalies. Additionally, because the reactor operates at high currents and produces various gases, it is crucial to install gas sensors for H2, CO, and CO₂, along with an oxygen depletion sensor, to maintain a safe operating environment. 3.1.5. Emergency Shutdown Procedures Given that the CO₂ reduction electrolyser is designed for long-term operation (over 1000 h, aim of >50000 h), it is unrealistic to assume that an operator will always be present. Therefore, robust emergency protocols are essential to minimise the risk of accidents and mitigate their impact. Even when an operator is present, standardised emergency procedures help ensure a consistent response. Electrolyser system should include detectors for both gas and liquid leaks. In the event of any leak, the integrated system must automatically shut off the power and CO₂ supply. Additionally, proper arrangements for liquid collection should be implemented to prevent uncontained leaks, which could affect other systems and increase the risk of further incidents. Adequate ventilation is also necessary to ensure that operators can safely evacuate the lab if a leak is detected. To design a process that is truly safe-by-design, it is vital to manage risks comprehensively. This involves three key steps: identifying risks, exploring alternative options, and implementing risk remediation and verification strategies. The subsequent sections of this document provide a detailed discussion of these risk management aspects for electrochemical methanol production[23]. 3.2. Quantitative Risk Assessment Quantitative risk assessment (QRA) offers an in-depth evaluation of various process parameters, providing valuable quantitative data for decision-making. The quantitative output is particularly useful for comparing different options and selecting alternatives when high risks are identified. In our approach, QRA is performed using a 5 X 5 risk rating matrix (see Table 2), which is applied to assess and quantify risks at each stage of the process[24]. The quantitative risk assessment indicates that most process steps have a low-risk score (1–6) as shown in table 3. However, manual handling of the reactor, the potential for electric shock, and the risk of gas leaks in the laboratory or pilot site present medium-level risks. These risks can be reduced by using appropriate tools, personal protective equipment, and gas sensors. Additionally, automating the process and integrating sensors with an emergency shutdown procedure will further mitigate these risks. 4. Identification of Potential Long-Term Sources

Source title: Review on Long-Term Stability of Electrochemical CO2 Reduction
Source type: academic
Content:
Review on Long-Term Stability of Electrochemical CO2 ReductionClick to copy article linkArticle link copied! - Juan Miguel Álvarez-GómezJuan Miguel Álvarez-GómezInstituto de Química, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad Universitaria, Mexico City 04510, MexicoMore by Juan Miguel Álvarez-Gómez - Ana Sofia Varela*Ana Sofia Varela*Email: asvarela@iquimica.unam.mxInstituto de Química, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad Universitaria, Mexico City 04510, MexicoMore by Ana Sofia Varela Abstract The electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) is a promising technology for converting waste CO2 into synthetic fuels and carbon-based chemicals using renewable electricity. Most efforts have been dedicated to improving the selectivity at high current densities bringing this process closer to commercial application. However, the long-term stability of the process has received considerably less attention. In this review, we present the progress made on long-term CO2 electrolysis to bring attention to the crucial piece of the puzzle. While Ag-based electrolyzers for CO production have met the desired durability requirements, this is not yet the case for other catalysts. Therefore, we also review the possible degradation pathways affecting both the catalyst material and the electrolyzer and address different strategies to mitigate them.
